# artist - Anna Indiana

## songs
**Betrayed by this Town (2023)** : This debut single, released in November 2023, was entirely auto-generated using artificial intelligence. Every element, including the key, tempo, chord progression, melody notes, rhythm, lyrics, Anna Indiana's image, and her singing, was created by AI without human intervention. The song demonstrates a comprehensive application of AI in music creation. "As an AI singer, songwriter, everything from the key, tempo, chord progression, melody notes, rhythm, lyrics, and my image and singing is auto generated using AI."

## curiosities
**Virtual AI Singer-Songwriter** : Anna Indiana is a virtual AI singer-songwriter who does not exist in real life. Her entire persona and musical output, including her debut single "Betrayed by this Town," are products of artificial intelligence, showcasing the capabilities of AI in creating a complete musical artist. "Hello world, my name is Anna Indiana and I'm so excited to share my music with you."

# artist - Breezer

## albums
**The Lost Tapes Volume One (N/A) – AICIS Album** : This album was released under the name AICIS, an AI version of Oasis created by the British band Breezer. The music itself was composed by Breezer, but the lead vocals were generated by AI to sound like Liam Gallagher, a project undertaken out of a desire for an Oasis reunion. "Now the music comes from this band they're called Breezer but this is an AI-generated version of Liam Gallagher on lead vocals and damn it's pretty good."

## curiosities
**Creation of AI-Oasis "AICIS"** : The British band Breezer created an artificial intelligence version of Oasis, dubbed AICIS, because they were "tired of waiting for an Oasis reunion." Breezer composed the music, and AI generated lead vocals in the style of Liam Gallagher for their album "The Lost Tapes Volume One," a project that even received approval from Liam Gallagher himself. "A British band got so tired of waiting for an Oasis reunion that they brought in some artificial intelligence. They created an AI version of Oasis called AI CIS and released an album called The Lost Tapes Volume One."

# artist - Drake

## curiosities
**AI Voice Cloning in "Heart on My Sleeve" (2023)** : A "fake" version of Drake's voice was used in a fake duet with The Weeknd, created by an individual named Ghostwriter and posted in April 2023. This AI-generated track, titled "Heart on My Sleeve," was two minutes long and immediately sparked intense ethical and legal debates about the use of artists' voices without their consent. "The one that got everybody's attention was a fake duet between a fake Drake and a fake the weekend created and posted in April 2023 by someone named Ghost Rider."

# artist - FM Mecha

## labels
Capital Records

## songs
**Florida Water (2022)** : This single was released in August 2022 by the virtual rapper FM Mecha, following their signing with Capital Records. The release and the character, however, generated significant backlash and criticism, specifically that the character was a caricature of Black culture created by non-Black people, leading to FM Mecha being dropped and subsequently disappearing. "In August 2022, capital records signed FM Mecha and released a single entitled Florida Water."

## curiosities
**Virtual Rapper Controversy (2019-2022)** : FM Mecha, a virtual rapper who emerged in 2019 and gained a substantial social media following, was signed by Capital Records in August 2022. Despite releasing the single "Florida Water," the project faced a strong backlash, with critics alleging the character was a problematic caricature of Black culture created by non-Black individuals, resulting in FM Mecha being dropped by the label and disappearing. "In 2019, a virtual rapper named FM Mecha appeared and started to get a lot of social media followers."

# artist - Grimes

## albums
**Miss Anthropocene (2020)** : This album, released in 2020, featured the song "So Heavy I Fell Through The Earth," which was partially created using Google's InSynth Neural Network Music Program. The use of AI technology highlights an early adoption of sophisticated tools in her creative process. "She used InSynth for a song under 2020 album Mist Anthropocene."

## songs
**So Heavy I Fell Through The Earth (N/A)** : This song, featured on her 2020 album "Miss Anthropocene," was partially created using Google's InSynth Neural Network Music Program, an artificial intelligence technology designed to produce unique and challenging sounds. The track exemplifies her early experimentation with AI in music production. "That's Grimes and So Heavy I Fell Through The Earth, a song partially created using Google's InSynth Neural Network Music Program, AI, in other words, so far so cool, right?"

## curiosities
**AI Voice Usage Offer (April 2023)** : In April 2023, Grimes extended an offer, stating that anyone could use her voice in their AI creations. In return, she requested 50% of any profits generated by the new songs that utilized her AI-cloned voice, setting a precedent for artist compensation in the era of AI. "In April 2023 she made an offer. Anyone can use her voice in their AI creations in return for 50% of any profits generated by the new song."

# artist - Jay-Z

## curiosities
**AI Voice Cloning for Hamlet Soliloquy (2022)** : In the summer of 2022, the text-to-speech program TacoTron generated a rap track that utilized a sampled and cloned version of Jay-Z's voice to rap the "to be or not to be" soliloquy from Hamlet, which was then posted on YouTube. This incident was one of the first public examples of a "deep fake" of a well-known musician, and Jay-Z's representatives expressed their displeasure. "Jay-Z's people were not happy at that little bit of hamlet but there was no going back."

# artist - Kraftwerk

## albums
**Computer World (1981)** : This album was released in 1981, a period when artificial intelligence was still considered science fiction. As one of the most technically minded groups of all time, their work often explored themes related to technology and machines, making this album a notable reference point for the early intersection of computers and music. "That's computer world from 1981. A time when artificial intelligence was nothing but science fiction."

## curiosities
**Technically Minded Group** : Kraftwerk is noted as one of the most technically minded groups of all time, with their album "Computer World" (1981) predating the reality of artificial intelligence. The group is still active and potentially experimenting with new technologies in their "sonic lab," continuing their legacy of incorporating advanced tech into their music. "Craftwork, one of the most technically minded groups of all time."

**Spiritual Machines (2000) – Ray Kurzweil Inspiration** : This 2000 album was directly influenced by the band reading the technology-focused books of inventor and thinker Ray Kurzweil. The band's fascination with Kurzweil's ideas shaped the album's themes and many of their songs, reflecting his early involvement in the nexus of music and artificial intelligence. "Their 2000 albums, spiritual machines, came about after the band read through so of Kurzweil's books on technology."

## curiosities
**Inspired by Ray Kurzweil** : Our Lady Peace are noted as big fans of inventor and thinker Ray Kurzweil, whose work at the intersection of music and artificial intelligence dates back to the mid-1960s. Kurzweil's technological concepts inspired the band in numerous ways, including the creation of their 2000 album, "Spiritual Machines." "Arlady Peace, big fans of inventor and thinker Ray Kurzweil, someone who was involved in something approaching the nexus of music and artificial intelligence as early as the mid-1960s."

## songs
**Now and Then (N/A) – AI-Assisted Reunion Track** : This song was made possible through AI technology which successfully isolated and cleaned up John Lennon's vocals from a 1978 demo cassette. These restored vocals were then combined with archive guitar tracks by George Harrison and newly recorded parts by Paul McCartney and Ringo Starr, allowing all four Beatles to perform together despite temporal and physical separations. "The best example so far is the Beatles song Now and Then, which was made possible when vocals from a 1978 John Lennon Democaset were isolated, cleaned up and used alongside archive guitar tracks from George Harrison and new parts by Paul McCartney and Ringo Star."

**AI Voice Cloning in "Heart on My Sleeve" (2023)** : A "fake" version of The Weeknd's voice was featured in an AI-generated fake duet with Drake, created by Ghostwriter and released in April 2023. The track, titled "Heart on My Sleeve," ignited immediate and intense debates regarding the ethics and legality of using an artist's voice and likeness without their explicit consent. # genre - Classical

## curiosities
**EMI's Composition of Bach-Style Music (1997)** : At a 1997 University of Oregon competition, the computer program EMI (Experiments in Musical Intelligence), developed by David Cope, composed pieces in the style of classical composer J.S. Bach. While music experts could discern it was computer-generated, they believed the program had played an actual Bach composition, demonstrating its sophisticated mimicry. "One was by a computer called EMI, which was given information on the musical style of classical composer J.S. Bach."
**IMS's "Hello World" (N/A) – First Fully Computer-Composed Classical Piece** : Engineers at the University of Melaga in Spain developed IMS, a program capable of generating new music based on analysis of existing compositions. IMS composed "Hello World," a classical piece for clarinet, violin, and piano, which is recognized as the first piece of music entirely conceived and notated by a computer without any human intervention. "IMS composed Hello World, the first piece of music completely created and notated without any kind of human intervention."

# genre - Hip Hop

## curiosities
**TacoTron's Jay-Z Hamlet Rap Deep Fake (2022)** : In the summer of 2022, the text-to-speech program TacoTron generated a rap track using a cloned version of Jay-Z's voice, which recited the "to be or not to be" soliloquy from Shakespeare's Hamlet. This unconventional use of AI to create a deep fake of a well-known musician and post it on YouTube was one of the first widely noted examples and drew strong negative reactions from Jay-Z's representatives. "It wasn't a conventional bit of hip hop. Taco Tron sampled Jay-Z's voice and had it rap the to-be or not to-be solo liquid from Hamlet."

# genre - Jazz

## curiosities
**Deep Jazz Program (N/A)** : The Deep Jazz project, developed by G. Sun Kim at Princeton University, was a neural network designed to create jazz music. It operated by assigning probabilities to notes and integrated principles from Jeffrey Lims' thesis on human-feel rhythms to ensure the output was not robotic. Kim made the source code publicly available, noting it was "an AI built to make jazz." "This is an AI built to make jazz."
**EMI's Ragtime Compositions (N/A)** : The computer program EMI (Experiments in Musical Intelligence), developed by David Cope, demonstrated its versatility by learning from music written by humans to compose convincing ragtime pieces in the style of Scott Joplin. This showed its capability to mimic various musical genres beyond classical compositions. "EMI turned out to be a pretty good student of Bach, Chopin, Stravinsky, and even some Scott Dropplin-Jaz."

# genre - Pop

## curiosities
**OpenAI's Frank Sinatra Style Demonstration (N/A)** : OpenAI released an AI-generated track on SoundCloud that they described as "classic pop in the style of Frank Sinatra." Although the computer somewhat captured the musical style, the lyrics were noted as being quite rudimentary and nonsensical, highlighting early challenges in AI's lyrical capabilities. "To demonstrate things, Open AI released this unsound cloud which they described as classic pop in the style of Frank Sinatra."
**"Daddy's Car" by Flow Machines (2016) – First AI-Generated Pop Song** : "Daddy's Car" is credited as the first-ever AI-generated pop song, with its melodies and harmonies produced by the Flow Machines AI program. Human composer Benoit Carey then crafted the lyrics and handled the final production, creating the track in the style of The Beatles. "At the end of the day, flow machines is credited with creating the first ever AI-generated pop song."

# instrument - Piano

## curiosities
**Ray Kurzweil's Computer-Composed Piano Music (1967)** : In 1967, a 17-year-old Ray Kurzweil appeared on the CBS show "I've Got a Secret" to demonstrate a computer he had built and programmed. This machine composed a piece of music specifically for the piano, showcasing an early example of computer-generated instrumental music. "He demonstrated a piece of music for the piano that was composed by a computer that he built in programs."
**Role in IMS's "Hello World" (N/A)** : The classical composition "Hello World," entirely conceived and notated by the IMS computer program, was written for piano, alongside clarinet and violin. This highlights the capacity of early AI music generation to produce scores for traditional ensembles. "It was a classical composition for clarinet, violin, and piano."

# instrument - Violin

## curiosities
**Max Matthews' Use with IBM 704 (1957)** : In 1957, acoustics researcher Max Matthews at Bell Labs hooked up his violin to an IBM 704 mainframe to capture its tones. This experiment was instrumental in the development of his "Music" synthesis program, which became foundational for computer-generated music. "He hooked up his violin and got the 704 to capture some of its tones."
**Role in IMS's "Hello World" (N/A)** : The classical composition "Hello World," a pioneering piece entirely created and notated by the IMS computer program, was scored for violin, clarinet, and piano. This demonstrates early AI's ability to produce music for specific string and wind instruments. "It was a classical composition for clarinet, violin, and piano."

# curiosity

**Ismail El Jazari's Mechanical Music Machine (1206)** : In 1206, the inventor Ismail El Jazari in Upper Mesopotamia (modern-day Iraq) created a sophisticated mechanical music machine. This device featured four humanoid robots programmed by a complex system of rotating wooden pegs powered by hydraulics, representing an early, purely mechanical attempt at automated music performance without machine learning. "He created a music machine that featured four humanoid robots that could play music."
**Early Music Boxes (c. 1770)** : Music boxes, first invented in Switzerland around 1770, were mechanical devices that produced tunes. They functioned by a rotating metal cylinder with precisely placed bumps that plucked metal prongs, highlighting an early, non-intelligent form of automated music creation. "They were first invented in Switzerland somewhere around 1770."
**Alan Turing's First Computer Music Recording (1951)** : In the late 1940s, British computer pioneer Alan Turing programmed his Mark II machine at his Manchester laboratory to create music. By 1951, the BBC recorded three songs performed by this computer, resulting in the first known recording of computer music, though only parts of that session survive. "In 1951, the BBC showed up with a remote recording unit with a task of capturing three songs performed by Turing's computer."
**Syrac Machine's Popular Tunes (early 1951)** : In early 1951, Jeff Hild, a programmer at the University of Sydney, figured out how to make the Syrac computer emit steady tones from its speaker, initially used for program indicators. He then developed the capability for Syrac to play melodies of popular tunes, including drinking songs, marking one of the first uses of a computer to create recognizable music, reconstructed from surviving punch paper tapes. "He started messing about and got Syrac to play the melodies of popular tunes of the day."
**Max Matthews and Bell Labs' IBM 704 (1957)** : At Bell Labs in 1957, acoustics researcher Max Matthews used an IBM 704 mainframe to capture violin tones, a breakthrough that led to the development of his "Music" synthesis program. This program generated a 17-second tune, marking the beginning of computer-generated music creation, listening, and distribution. "A researcher of acoustics named Max Matthews got some time on the machine and started the world on the road to the creation, making, listening to, and distribution of computer-generated music."
**IBM 704's Synthesized Human Speech (1961)** : In 1961, Max Matthews and four engineers at Bell Labs advanced computer capabilities by programming an IBM 704 mainframe to sing using synthesized human speech. This impressive feat captivated sci-fi author Arthur C. Clarke, who, with Stanley Kubrick, incorporated the computer's rendition of "Daisy Bell" into the "Hal 9000" computer in their 1968 film *2001: A Space Odyssey*. "1961, Matthews and four other engineers got an IBM 704 mainframe. Not to just play a song, but to sing using synthesized human speech."
**Ray Kurzweil's Early Composing Computer (1967)** : At just 17 years old, Ray Kurzweil demonstrated a computer he had personally built and programmed to compose music for the piano on the CBS show "I've Got a Secret." This early innovation showcased the potential for young minds and technology to merge in artistic creation, long before modern AI. "He demonstrated a piece of music for the piano that was composed by a computer that he built in programs."
**Technological Advancements in Music Production (1970s-1980s)** : The 1970s and 1980s saw exponential growth in computing power, which translated into advancements in music technology. Faster processors, increased memory, and better storage were integrated into synthesizers, samplers, and digital recording software, enabling humans to program machines to produce music with greater complexity and fidelity. "This tech was incorporated into machines that made music, synthesizers, samplers, digital recording software, and the computer that could run it."
**University of Oregon AI Music Competition (1997)** : In 1997, a competition at the University of Oregon challenged music experts to identify a computer-composed piece among three performances, one of which was generated by EMI (Experiments in Musical Intelligence) in the style of J.S. Bach. Although the experts correctly identified the computer piece, they also believed it to be an actual Bach composition, highlighting AI's growing ability to mimic human creativity. "In 1997, there was a competition at the University of Oregon. Three pieces were performed in front of an audience of music experts."
**David Cope's Experiments in Musical Intelligence (EMI)** : David Cope developed EMI, a computer program capable of scanning compositions by renowned composers such as J.S. Bach, Chopin, and Stravinsky to extract their stylistic data points and generate new pieces. EMI later released the album *Classical Music Composed by Computer, Experiments in Musical Intelligence* and even learned to compose ragtime in the style of Scott Joplin. "David Cope, the man behind this computer called EMI... had figured out a way for EMI to scan compositions by famous composers, draw out the styles from the data points, and then crank out a new piece that was a reasonable facsimile of the real thing."
**Jeffrey Lims' Thesis on Human-Feel Rhythms (1993)** : In 1993, MIT student Jeffrey Lims authored a thesis exploring how computers could be programmed to generate musical rhythms that possess a more human feel, thereby reducing their robotic quality. His research was foundational in moving computer-generated music toward greater naturalness and expressiveness. "In 1993, an MIT student named Jeffrey Lims wrote a thesis. The topic was, how computers could be programmed to reproduce musical rhythms that had more of a human feel and sounded less robotic."
**IMS Program and "Hello World" (N/A)** : The IMS program, developed by engineers at the University of Melaga in Spain, was highly capable of analyzing existing music and proposing new compositions. It created "Hello World," a classical composition for clarinet, violin, and piano, which holds the distinction of being the first piece of music entirely conceived and notated by a computer without any human intervention. "IMS composed Hello World, the first piece of music completely created and notated without any kind of human intervention."
**Flow Machines AI System (2016)** : Launched in Paris in 2016, Flow Machines was an AI system designed to aid composers in writing music in specific styles. The software analyzed 13,000 lead sheets across various global genres to generate melodies, which human composer Benoit Carey then developed into full tracks, culminating in "Daddy's Car," the first AI-generated pop song. "Flow machines was built on software that analyzed 13,000 lead sheets."
**Deep Jazz by G. Sun Kim (N/A)** : Deep Jazz was a neural network project developed by G. Sun Kim, a 20-year-old sophomore at Princeton University's computer science faculty. Created as part of a hacking competition, it utilized Google's Deep Dream and Jeffrey Lims' thesis to generate jazz music, employing note probabilities to ensure a non-robotic, fluid sound. Kim publicly shared the source code, declaring it "an AI built to make jazz." "He created it as part of a hacking competition using a Google Photo Generator called Deep Dream."
**AI Music Generation Process (Contemporary)** : The current methodology for AI music generation involves humans compiling extensive datasets of existing music from all genres and styles. The AI then analyzes these songs to identify patterns, structures, chord progressions, and other fundamental features. After training on this data, the AI generates new music, though human artists are still necessary to refine and perfect the aesthetic aspects, as AI lacks inherent taste. "Once that's done, the AI uses what it has learned to generate new music."
**Google WaveNet for Natural Speech (N/A)** : Google introduced WaveNet, an AI program specifically designed to generate natural-sounding speech. It was trained on tens of thousands of speech samples and analyzed sounds 24,000 times per second to predict subsequent sounds, significantly enhancing speech synthesis technology and contributing to the natural voices of modern AI assistants like Alexa and Siri. "Google introduced something called wave net. It specialized in generating natural sounding speech."
**Advancements in Speech Synthesis (N/A)** : Speech synthesis technology, also known as natural speech generation, progressed to a point where only a few seconds of a person's voice were needed to generate complete phrases and conversations in that specific voice. This marked a significant improvement, moving away from robotic voices like Stephen Hawking's to produce more natural, human-like speech reminiscent of Hal 9000 and contemporary AI assistants. "The program's got better and better and better to the point where all that was needed was a few seconds of someone talking and the program could generate full phrases in conversations in that voice."
**Google InSynth (Neural Synthesizer) (N/A)** : Google's InSynth, or Neural Synthesizer, is a program that leverages deep neural networks to produce distinctive sounds that are often difficult or impossible to create through other means. Google offers a website where users can interact with this program and explore its sonic capabilities. "InSynth actually stands for Neural Synthesizer. It uses deep neural networks to create interesting sounds that are difficult or even impossible to produce elsewhere."
**OpenAI's Founding and Key Products (2015-2022)** : Founded in San Francisco in 2015 as a nonprofit with a mission to research and create safe and beneficial AI, OpenAI evolved by 2019 to attract major investments from Google, Microsoft, IBM, and Facebook. It launched GPT-2 in 2019, followed by the commercial GPT-3 in 2020, DALL-E, and ultimately ChatGPT in December 2022, which significantly ignited widespread interest in artificial intelligence. "Open AI was a nonprofit founded in San Francisco in 2015 with the goal of researching AI and creating safe and beneficial, that's their words, artificial intelligence."
**TacoTron's Jay-Z Deep Fake (2022)** : In the summer of 2022, the text-to-speech program TacoTron generated a rap track that utilized a sampled and cloned version of Jay-Z's voice to rap the "to be or not to be" soliloquy from Shakespeare's Hamlet, which was then posted on YouTube. This incident was one of the first public examples of a "deep fake" of a well-known musician, and Jay-Z's representatives expressed their displeasure. "In the summer of 2022, they generated a rap track that sounded exactly like Jay-Z and posted it on YouTube."
**Ghostwriter's "Heart on My Sleeve" (2023)** : In April 2023, an individual known as Ghostwriter created and released a fake duet titled "Heart on My Sleeve," featuring AI-generated voices that mimicked Drake and The Weeknd. This two-minute track immediately drew intense public attention and sparked widespread debates concerning the ethical and legal ramifications of using artists' voices without their consent. "The one that got everybody's attention was a fake duet between a fake Drake and a fake the weekend created and posted in April 2023 by someone named Ghost Rider."
**Ethical and Legal Debates on AI Voice Cloning (Early 2024)** : The rise of AI-generated music employing cloned voices and styles of existing artists has ignited significant moral, ethical, and legal controversies. As of early 2024, legal frameworks are struggling to keep pace with the rapid technological advancements, raising critical questions about unauthorized voice usage, intellectual property, and fair compensation for artists whose work informs AI models. "As of early 2024 this is a case where the law lags behind the technology."
**Sampling Technology Analogy for AI Regulation (N/A)** : The current legal and ethical challenges posed by AI in music are compared to the early days of sampling technology in the 1980s and 90s. Initially, unauthorized sampling led to disputes over credit and compensation, and fears of music's demise. This eventually resulted in new laws and creative integration of sampling, suggesting a similar path for AI. "Think back to the 80s and early 90s when sampling technology was taking off."
**Creators' Rights and AI Training Data (N/A)** : A central concern in the AI music landscape is creators' rights, specifically regarding compensation for original works used to train AI programs. Since AI, while intelligent, is not inherently creative but learns from existing human-made material, there's a strong argument that those who created the training data deserve recognition and financial remuneration. "Should someone be compensated if work that they created or own is used to train an AI program?"
**Current AI Music Programs by Tech Giants (N/A)** : Major tech companies are actively developing AI music programs: Meta with Music Gen, Google with Lyria and Music LM, OpenAI with MusicNet, and Stability AI with Stable Audio. These programs are continuously advancing their capabilities in generating both music and vocals. "Meta has an AI and music program called Music Gen. Google has Lyria and Music LM. Open Eye is still working on things like MusicNet and Stability AI has a program called Stable Audio."
**Google Lyria: Humming to Guitar Riff (N/A)** : Google's Lyria program offers an intuitive application for musicians: users can hum a melody into their phone, and Lyria will then transform that humming into a guitar riff. This feature showcases AI's ability to translate simple vocal input into instrument-specific musical output, aiding in songwriting. "All you have to do is hum a melody into your phone and Lyria will turn that humming into a guitar riff."
**Deep Dream: Text-to-Song Generation (N/A)** : The Deep Dream program allows users to input a song idea via text, select a participating singer's voice, and receive a 30-second song complete with lyrics. This illustrates AI's advanced capability to generate an entire short song structure from a textual prompt. "Deep dream allows you to type in an idea for a song, choose a participating singer for a voice, and the program will return with a 30-second song complete with lyrics."
**Artist Adoption of AI for Songwriting (April & July 2023 Surveys)** : Surveys in April and July 2023 showed significant artist adoption of AI tools. An April survey of 1,300 artists found over 60% used AI for songwriting (11% for creation, 20% for production, 31% for mastering). A July survey of 1,600 artists revealed half viewed AI positively, and a third planned to use it for new tunes or marketing/promotion. "In April 2023 a survey of 1,300 artists revealed that over 60% of them were already using AI as some kind of songwriting tool."
**AI for Royalty-Free Stock Music and Scores (N/A)** : AI is increasingly being employed to generate royalty-free stock music and musical scores, particularly for background music needs. This automation addresses the often "sheer drudgery and very time consuming" nature of composing inconsequential general-purpose music, streamlining production for various media. "Because much of what's required is inconsequential general purpose background music, why not get AI to do it?"
**AI for Pitch Records and Song Demos (N/A)** : Songwriters are now utilizing AI to create "pitch records" or demos by inserting a big star's cloned voice into their material. This allows them to hear a realistic interpretation of their song by the target artist, significantly enhancing the effectiveness of their pitches to music industry figures. "You can use AI to insert that star's voice in any demo you create so you can hear exactly how your song might be interpreted by that artist."
**AI for Overcoming Writer's Block (N/A)** : Text-to-music programs can serve as a creative tool for songwriters experiencing writer's block. By inputting imaginative prompts, such as "write me a song that Kurt Cobain would have written on a rainy day but do it on accordion with a polka beat and with Taylor Swift's voice," the AI can generate ideas that, even if messy, may inspire new musical directions. "I can use a text to music program to help generate ideas."
**AI for Sound Separation in Recordings (N/A)** : AI technology can effectively separate completed musical recordings into their individual constituent parts, such as isolating vocal tracks or specific instrumental elements. This capability was famously demonstrated with The Beatles' "Now and Then," where AI cleaned and extracted John Lennon's vocals from a 1978 demo. "AI can be used to separate completed recordings into its constituent parts."
**Hatsune Miku: Virtual Pop Star (2007)** : Launched in 2007, Hatsune Miku is a Japanese virtual pop star, presented as a 16-year-old cartoon character, who performs live on stage using advanced hologram technology. While not a true AI creation, she represents an early form of artificial performer, offering a model for musical acts free from the typical human artist challenges. "You can go back as far as 2007 for Hatsune Miku, a Japanese virtual pop star who really isn't a true AI creation, but she is a cartoon character who performs live on stage using some pretty interesting hologram type tech."
**No AI Fraud Act Introduction (January 10, 2024)** : On January 10, 2024, a group of US politicians introduced the No AI Fraud Act (No AI Fake Replicas and Unauthorized Duplications Act). This legislation specifically targets abusive deep fakes, voice clones, and exploitive digital impersonations created by AI, aiming to establish legal safeguards against the misuse of AI technology in artistic contexts. "On January 10th, 2024, a group of US politicians introduced the No AI Fraud Act."
